title: Fruit scones
subtitle: Light, tall British scones with sultanas, served with jam and cream.
excerpt: Scones made with a light hand and a hot oven: flour, butter rubbed in quickly, milk added cold, cut thick and baked fast. Risen, fluffy, and split for jam and clotted cream. Makes eight to ten.
sourceNotes: Fruit scones. Scones appear in British and Scottish baking manuals from the 18th century onward. The cream scone became associated with the West Country cream tea tradition in the 19th century. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) contains several scone recipes. The debate over whether jam or cream goes first (Devon vs Cornwall) is documented; the recipe takes no position.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked scones. Warm in a 180°C oven for 8 minutes from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. Dough can be cut and frozen unbaked on a tray, then transferred to a bag.

content.text: Scones rise tall when the oven is hot and the handling is minimal. Two rules: do not overwork the dough after the milk goes in, and cut straight down with the cutter without twisting. Twisting seals the edges and prevents the scone from rising. Press the cutter down and lift straight up.

items.prepNote: or whole milk with 1 tsp lemon juice

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C. Rub {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter into {{self-raising-flour}} of flour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{caster-sugar}} of sugar until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Stir in {{sultanas}} of sultanas. Add {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk and mix quickly with a fork until the dough just comes together. Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and pat to 2.5 cm thick. Cut out rounds with a floured 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Re-gather and cut the trimmings.

Place on a lined baking tray. Brush the tops with {{eggs}} beaten egg, avoiding the sides. Bake at 220°C for 10 to 12 minutes until risen and deep golden. Cool on a wire rack. Split and serve with jam and clotted cream.

items.cause: Dough overworked, cutter twisted, or oven not hot enough.
items.fix: Mix only until the dough holds together. Cut straight down. Preheat the oven fully to 220°C before the scones go in.

items.symptom: Scones spread sideways instead of rising
items.cause: Dough too wet, or not cut thick enough.
items.fix: Pat to 2.5 cm. Wet dough loses its structure; if the mixture looks too sticky, add 1 tbsp more flour.

items.symptom: Bottom burns before top is cooked
items.cause: Tray on too low a shelf.
items.fix: Bake on the middle or upper-middle shelf. Scones bake fast and need the top heat as much as the bottom.

content.text: Scones are the centrepiece of the British cream tea, eaten throughout the afternoon from Cornwall to Edinburgh. The cream tea tradition is specific to the West Country and Scotland, though scones appear everywhere. Homemade scones, eaten fresh, are so far above the supermarket equivalent that they might as well be a different food.
